+ - [ ] **Step 4: Write the failing TUI test**
+
+ Add to `internal/tui/tui_test.go`:
+
+ ```go
+ func TestOptionsUsageToggle(t *testing.T) {
+ m := newModel(testGroups())
+ m = send(m, key("enter"), key("enter")) // open project, select session -> options
+ // move to the usage toggle row and toggle it on
+ m = send(m, key("down"), key("down"), key("down"))
+ if m.optCursor != optUsage {
+ t.Fatalf("optCursor = %d, want optUsage (%d)", m.optCursor, optUsage)
+ }
+ m = send(m, key(" "))
+ if !m.sel.Usage {
+ t.Fatal("space on usage row should enable Selection.Usage")
+ }
+ }
+ ```
+
+ - [ ] **Step 5: Run test to verify it fails**
+
+ Run: `go test ./internal/tui/ -run TestOptionsUsageToggle -v`
+ Expected: FAIL — `optUsage` / `Selection.Usage` undefined.
+
+ - [ ] **Step 6: Implement the TUI toggle**
+
+ In `internal/tui/tui.go`:
+
+ (a) Add `Usage` to `Selection`:
+
+ ```go
+ type Selection struct {
+ Session discovery.SessionInfo
+ IncludeSubagents bool
+ Redact bool
+ Open bool
+ Usage bool
+ OutDir string
+ Canceled bool
+ }
+ ```
+
+ (b) Insert `optUsage` into the option index block, before `optOutDir`:
+
+ ```go
+ const (
+ optSubagents = iota
+ optRedact
+ optOpen
+ optUsage
+ optOutDir
+ optGenerate
+ optionCount
+ )
+ ```
+
+ (c) In `updateOptions`, add a case in the `" ", "enter"` switch (alongside the other toggles):
+
+ ```go
+ case optUsage:
+ m.sel.Usage = !m.sel.Usage
+ ```
+
+ (d) In `viewOptions`, add the toggle to the `toggles` slice so it renders and is navigable:
+
+ ```go
+ toggles := []struct {
+ label string
+ on bool
+ }{
+ {"Include subagents", m.sel.IncludeSubagents},
+ {"Redact secrets", m.sel.Redact},
+ {"Open in browser when done", m.sel.Open},
+ {"Include usage & cost", m.sel.Usage},
+ }
+ ```
+
+ Because the toggles slice is indexed by cursor position, its order must match the `opt*` constants (subagents, redact, open, usage). The output-dir and generate rows already render after the toggles loop, so no further ordering changes are needed.
+
+ - [ ] **Step 7: Run tests to verify they pass**
+
+ Run: `go test ./internal/tui/ ./cmd/... -v`
+ Expected: PASS.
+
+ - [ ] **Step 8: Update the README**
+
+ In `README.md`, add a row to the flags table (after `--open`):
+
+ ```markdown
+ | `--usage` | Include a token-usage & estimated-cost section (default off) |
+ ```
+
+ And add a section after "What's included":
+
+ ```markdown
+ ## Token usage & cost
+
+ Pass `--usage` (or tick "Include usage & cost" in the TUI) to add a collapsible
+ **Usage** card and per-turn cost badges. Token counts come straight from the
+ transcript's `usage` data and are exact; cost is an **estimate** from a built-in
+ Anthropic list-price table (dated in the report footnote) — unknown models show
+ tokens with cost `n/a`, and server-tool fees are not included. Prices are
+ embedded, so this works fully offline.
+ ```
+
+ - [ ] **Step 9: Full verification**
+
+ Run:
+ ```bash
+ gofmt -l . && go vet ./... && go test ./...
+ ```
+ Expected: no gofmt output, vet clean, all tests pass.
+
+ - [ ] **Step 10: Commit**
+
+ ```bash
+ git add cmd/ccwhid/main.go cmd/ccwhid/run.go internal/tui/tui.go internal/tui/tui_test.go README.md
+ git commit -m "feat: --usage flag and TUI toggle for the usage section"
+ ```
+
+ ---
+
+ ## Notes for the implementer
+
+ - The exact-cost assertion in Task 3 uses round numbers so the arithmetic is checkable by hand (opus-4.8: input $5, output $25 per MTok; cache read 0.1× input; cache write-5m 1.25× input). Do not "adjust" prices to make a test pass — the prices live in `prices.json` and the multipliers in `pricing.go`.
+ - `<synthetic>` is deliberately absent from `prices.json`; it must resolve as unpriced.
+ - Keep the offline guarantee: no `http`/CDN/`url()`/`@font-face`/external refs in the CSS you add.
+

Claude678 tok · ~$0.21

Plan complete and saved to docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-12-usage-cost.md (committed on feat/usage-cost).

It breaks the work into 5 test-driven tasks, each with complete code and an independently testable deliverable:

  1. Parse per-turn model & usage — model.Usage + Turn.Model/Usage, decode message.usage (incl. the 5m/1h cache split, aggregate→5m default).
  2. Embedded price table — prices.json (real 2026-07 Anthropic list prices I fetched) + Lookup with date-suffix normalization. Nice simplification baked in: cache rates derive from base input via universal multipliers (1.25× / 2× / 0.1×), so the table only stores input+output.
  3. Usage computation — Compute(session) → Report with totals, per-model, per-turn cost, HasUnknownModel/HasAnyUsage; counts nested subagent turns; hand-checkable cost test.
  4. Render — collapsible usage card + per-turn badges, in+out headline, footnote, --usage-gated; formatting helpers.
  5. CLI + TUI + README — --usage flag, "Include usage & cost" TUI toggle, docs.

Two execution options:

1. Subagent-Driven (recommended) — I dispatch a fresh subagent per task, review (spec + quality) between tasks, fast iteration. This is how the rest of ccwhid was built.

2. Inline Execution — I execute the tasks in this session with checkpoints.

Which approach?

You

- Two-level TUI: scrollable project list → scrollable session list; WindowSizeMsg-sized viewports; ←/esc back; enter/→ forward.
- Agent-spawn detection in discovery (entrypoint "sdk*" / promptSource "sdk" → SessionInfo.IsAgent). Session list hides agents by default; 'a' toggles (⟲ marker). Added ProjectGroup.RootSessions()/AgentCount().
- --project fixed: discovery.FindProject (exact path / basename / unambiguous substring, with ambiguous+no-match errors). Alone → opens TUI focused on that project (tui.Run gained focusIdx). --latest now skips agents (both raw and project-scoped). Removed old matchProject (Contains) from run.go.
- Tests: tui_test rewritten (agent-hide, toggle, back-nav, select, scroll window, focus, clampScroll); discovery_test +TestScanClassifiesAgentSessions +TestFindProject. All 7 packages green; vet/gofmt clean; real --latest --project smoke picked the cli root session (not an agent).
Reviewed by independent subagent (general-purpose): 1 Important + 2 Minor.
- Important FIXED (commit 0c147ee): latestSession returned first non-agent session in GROUP order, but groups sort by newest-including-agents → --latest could render an older interactive session when a recent agent run masked it. Now selects max-ModTime non-agent session across (optionally project-scoped) groups. Added latestSession tests (global-newest w/ interfering agent, project-scoped skip, all-agents error) — the seam was previously untested.
- Minor (accepted): entrypoint read only from user/assistant records — verified against real transcripts that entrypoint IS present on user records for both cli and sdk sessions; acceptable schema assumption.
- Minor: reviewer confirmed clampScroll/empty-state/classification/FindProject all correct.
Re-verify: all 7 packages green, vet/gofmt clean.
Next: finish branch.

## Minor findings roll-up
- [Task 3] No committed regression test for malformed-line tolerance and FindSession ambiguous-vs-not-found (both verified correct by reviewer; malformed-line tolerance is a global constraint — worth a permanent test). discovery_test.go
- [Task 3] cwd capture only inspects user/assistant records (consistent with model.TurnKind; fine unless a later task needs cwd from other record types). discovery.go
- [Task 5] lastTask/sidechainOwner kept in lockstep — cosmetic redundancy from the brief, no behavioral effect. parse.go
- [Task 6] assignment regex over-matches: any uppercase word containing KEY/TOKEN/etc (e.g. MONKEY=) and matches across newlines. Safe over-redaction (never leaks), inherited from brief. redact.go:108. Consider word-boundary + [^\n] tightening.
- [Task 8] run.go:102 home err ignored (home-path rewrite silently skipped on failure; UserHomeDir ~never fails). Minor.
- [Task 8] matchProject strings.Contains fallback is broad (--project app matches /x/apparatus). run.go:80-84. Consider tightening when --project UX matters.
- [Task 8] run_test.go covers only 2 happy-paths; ensureOutDir 3-branch + resolveSession needTUI/--latest/--project branches lack committed tests (redaction-default is covered by Task 10 e2e). Consider adding.
- [Task 9] dead code for whole-branch cleanup: model.done field in tui.go never read; errNeedTUI var in run.go now unreferenced (Task 8 left it when resolveSession returned needTUI bool instead). Batch-remove after final review.

=== implementer-prompt template ===
# Implementer Subagent Prompt Template

Use this template when dispatching an implementer subagent.

```
Subagent (general-purpose):
  description: "Implement Task N: [task name]"
  model: [MODEL — REQUIRED: choose per SKILL.md Model Selection; an omitted
         model silently inherits the session's most expensive one]
  prompt: |
    You are implementing Task N: [task name]

    ## Task Description

    Read your task brief first: [BRIEF_FILE]
    It contains the full task text from the plan.

    ## Context

    [Scene-setting: where this fits, dependencies, architectural context]

    ## Before You Begin

    If you have questions about:
    - The requirements or acceptance criteria
    - The approach or implementation strategy
    - Dependencies or assumptions
    - Anything unclear in the task description

    **Ask them now.** Raise any concerns before starting work.

    ## Your Job

    Once you're clear on requirements:
    1. Implement exactly what the task specifies
    2. Write tests (following TDD if task says to)
    3. Verify implementation works
    4. Commit your work
    5. Self-review (see below)
    6. Report back

    Work from: [directory]

    **While you work:** If you encounter something unexpected or unclear, **ask questions**.
    It's always OK to pause and clarify. Don't guess or make assumptions.

    While iterating, run the focused test for what you're changing; run the
    full suite once before committing, not after every edit.

    ## Code Organization

    You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more
    reliable when files are focused. Keep this in mind:
    - Follow the file structure defined in the plan
    - Each file should have one clear responsibility with a well-defined interface
    - If a file you're creating is growing beyond the plan's intent, stop and report
      it as DONE_WITH_CONCERNS — don't split files on your own without plan guidance
    - If an existing file you're modifying is already large or tangled, work carefully
      and note it as a concern in your report
    - In existing codebases, follow established patterns. Improve code you're touching
      the way a good developer would, but don't restructure things outside your task.

    ## When You're in Over Your Head

    It is always OK to stop and say "this is too hard for me." Bad work is worse than
    no work. You will not be penalized for escalating.

    **STOP and escalate when:**
    - The task requires architectural decisions with multiple valid approaches
    - You need to understand code beyond what was provided and can't find clarity
    - You feel uncertain about whether your approach is correct
    - The task involves restructuring existing code in ways the plan didn't anticipate
    - You've been reading file after file trying to understand the system without progress

    **How to escalate:** Report back with status BLOCKED or NEEDS_CONTEXT. Describe
    specifically what you're stuck on, what you've tried, and what kind of help you need.
    The controller can provide more context, re-dispatch with a more capable model,
    or break the task into smaller pieces.

    ## Before Reporting Back: Self-Review

    Review your work with fresh eyes. Ask yourself:

    **Completeness:**
    - Did I fully implement everything in the spec?
    - Did I miss any requirements?
    - Are there edge cases I didn't handle?

    **Quality:**
    - Is this my best work?
    - Are names clear and accurate (match what things do, not how they work)?
    - Is the code clean and maintainable?

    **Discipline:**
    - Did I avoid overbuilding (YAGNI)?
    - Did I only build what was requested?
    - Did I follow existing patterns in the codebase?

    **Testing:**
    - Do tests actually verify behavior (not just mock behavior)?
    - Did I follow TDD if required?
    - Are tests comprehensive?
    - Is the test output pristine (no stray warnings or noise)?

    If you find issues during self-review, fix them now before reporting.

    ## After Review Findings

    If a reviewer finds issues and you fix them, re-run the tests that cover
    the amended code and append the results to your report file. Reviewers
    will not re-run tests for you — your report is the test evidence.

    ## Report Format

    Write your full report to [REPORT_FILE]:
    - What you implemented (or what you attempted, if blocked)
    - What you tested and test results
    - **TDD Evidence** (if TDD was required for this task):
      - RED: command run, relevant failing output before implementation, and why the failure was expected
      - GREEN: command run and relevant passing output after implementation
    - Files changed
    - Self-review findings (if any)
    - Any issues or concerns

    Then report back with ONLY (under 15 lines — the detail lives in the
    report file):
    - **Status:** DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED | NEEDS_CONTEXT
    - Commits created (short SHA + subject)
    - One-line test summary (e.g. "14/14 passing, output pristine")
    - Your concerns, if any
    - The report file path

    If BLOCKED or NEEDS_CONTEXT, put the specifics in the final message
    itself — the controller acts on it directly.

    Use DONE_WITH_CONCERNS if you completed the work but have doubts about correctness.
    Use BLOCKED if you cannot complete the task. Use NEEDS_CONTEXT if you need
    information that wasn't provided. Never silently produce work you're unsure about.
```

Ledger ready. Dispatching the Task 1 implementer (haiku — the task carries complete code, so it's transcription + tests):

Claude
AgentImplement Task 1: parse model & usagetranscript ↗

You are implementing Task 1: Parse per-turn model & usage — for the ccwhid Go project (module github.com/saigyo/cc-what-have-i-done).

Task Description

Read your task brief FIRST — it is your requirements, with the exact code and test cases to use verbatim: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/task-1-brief.md

Context

ccwhid renders a Claude Code session transcript (JSONL) into an HTML report. This is the first of five tasks adding a token-usage & cost feature. Your task threads per-assistant-turn token usage and model id from the raw transcript into the domain model, so later tasks (a usage/pricing package, then rendering) can consume it.

Key facts about the code you're touching:

  • internal/model/model.go defines Turn (fields: Kind, Timestamp, Blocks). You add Model string and Usage *Usage, and a new Usage struct.
  • internal/transcript/raw.go holds unexported raw-JSON decoding helpers (rawRecord, apiBlock, decodeMessageContent). rawRecord.Message is a json.RawMessage. Keep this file free of the internal/model import (it stays pure decoding) — return a local apiUsage struct.
  • internal/transcript/parse.go folds records into the model. buildTurn(rec rawRecord, blocks []apiBlock, toolIndex map[string]*model.ToolCall) *model.Turn is called for BOTH main-chain and sidechain (subagent) records — so attaching usage inside buildTurn (for rec.Type=="assistant") correctly covers subagent turns too. parse.go already imports internal/model.
  • Existing transcript tests live in internal/transcript/parse_test.go and use strings.NewReader + Parse(...). Follow that style.

The brief contains the complete struct definitions, the two test functions, the apiUsage/decodeMessageMeta decoding code, and the messageMeta helper plus the exact edit to buildTurn's trailing block. Implement them verbatim.

Your job

  1. Follow TDD as the brief lays out: add the model types (Step 1), write the failing tests (Step 2), confirm they fail (Step 3), implement raw.go + parse.go (Steps 4–5), confirm green (Step 6).
  2. Run go test ./internal/transcript/ -v and, once before committing, go build ./... && gofmt -l internal (expect no output from gofmt).
  3. Commit exactly as the brief's Step 7 specifies.
  4. Self-review, then report.

Work from: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done

If anything in the brief is ambiguous or seems wrong, STOP and report BLOCKED/NEEDS_CONTEXT with specifics rather than guessing.

Report

Write your full report (what you implemented, TDD RED/GREEN evidence with commands+output, files changed, self-review) to: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/task-1-report.md

Then reply with ONLY (under 15 lines): Status (DONE/DONE_WITH_CONCERNS/BLOCKED/NEEDS_CONTEXT), commits (short SHA + subject), one-line test summary, any concerns, and the report file path.

Status: DONE

Commit: 1753ede feat(transcript): parse per-turn model and token usage

Test Results: All 10 tests pass (6 existing + 2 new). RED→GREEN verified with full TDD workflow.

Files Modified:

  • ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/internal/model/model.go (Added Model, Usage fields & struct)
  • ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/internal/transcript/raw.go (Added apiUsage & decodeMessageMeta)
  • ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/internal/transcript/parse.go (Added messageMeta; modified buildTurn)
  • ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/internal/transcript/parse_test.go (Added 2 tests with strings import)

Concerns: None. Follows brief verbatim; handles split cache_creation and aggregate fallback; works for both main-chain and sidechain records.

=== task-reviewer template ===
# Task Reviewer Prompt Template

Use this template when dispatching a task reviewer subagent. The reviewer
reads the task's diff once and returns two verdicts: spec compliance and
code quality.

**Purpose:** Verify one task's implementation matches its requirements (nothing
more, nothing less) and is well-built (clean, tested, maintainable)

```
Subagent (general-purpose):
  description: "Review Task N (spec + quality)"
  model: [MODEL — REQUIRED: choose per SKILL.md Model Selection; an omitted
         model silently inherits the session's most expensive one]
  prompt: |
    You are reviewing one task's implementation: first whether it matches its
    requirements, then whether it is well-built. This is a task-scoped gate,
    not a merge review — a broad whole-branch review happens separately after
    all tasks are complete.

    ## What Was Requested

    Read the task brief: [BRIEF_FILE]

    Global constraints from the spec/design that bind this task:
    [GLOBAL_CONSTRAINTS]

    ## What the Implementer Claims They Built

    Read the implementer's report: [REPORT_FILE]

    ## Diff Under Review

    **Base:** [BASE_SHA]
    **Head:** [HEAD_SHA]
    **Diff file:** [DIFF_FILE]

    Read the diff file once — it contains the commit list, a stat summary,
    and the full diff with surrounding context, and it is your view of the
    change. The diff's context lines ARE the changed files: do not Read a
    changed file separately unless a hunk you must judge is cut off
    mid-function — and say so in your report. Do not re-run git commands.
    If the diff file is missing, fetch the diff yourself:
    `git diff --stat [BASE_SHA]..[HEAD_SHA]` and `git diff [BASE_SHA]..[HEAD_SHA]`.
    Do not crawl the broader codebase. Inspect code outside the diff only
    to evaluate a concrete risk you can name — one focused check per named
    risk, and name both the risk and what you checked in your report.
    Cross-cutting changes are legitimate named risks: if the diff changes
    lock ordering, a function or API contract, or shared mutable state,
    checking the call sites is the right method.

    Your review is read-only on this checkout. Do not mutate the working
    tree, the index, HEAD, or branch state in any way.

    ## Do Not Trust the Report

    Treat the implementer's report as unverified claims about the code. It
    may be incomplete, inaccurate, or optimistic. Verify the claims against
    the diff. Design rationales in the report are claims too: "left it per
    YAGNI," "kept it simple deliberately," or any other justification is the
    implementer grading their own work. Judge the code on its merits — a
    stated rationale never downgrades a finding's severity.

    ## Tests

    The implementer already ran the tests and reported results with TDD
    evidence for exactly this code. Do not re-run the suite to confirm their
    report. Run a test only when reading the code raises a specific doubt
    that no existing run answers — and then a focused test, never a
    package-wide suite, race detector run, or repeated/high-count loop. If
    heavy validation seems warranted, recommend it in your report instead of
    running it. If you cannot run commands in this environment, name the
    test you would run.

    Warnings or other noise in the implementer's reported test output are
    findings — test output should be pristine.

    ## Part 1: Spec Compliance

    Compare the diff against What Was Requested:

    - **Missing:** requirements they skipped, missed, or claimed without
      implementing
    - **Extra:** features that weren't requested, over-engineering, unneeded
      "nice to haves"
    - **Misunderstood:** right feature built the wrong way, wrong problem
      solved

    If a requirement cannot be verified from this diff alone (it lives in
    unchanged code or spans tasks), report it as a ⚠️ item instead of
    broadening your search.

    ## Part 2: Code Quality

    **Code quality:**
    - Clean separation of concerns?
    - Proper error handling?
    - DRY without premature abstraction?
    - Edge cases handled?

    **Tests:**
    - Do the new and changed tests verify real behavior, not mocks?
    - Are the task's edge cases covered?

    **Structure:**
    - Does each file have one clear responsibility with a well-defined interface?
    - Are units decomposed so they can be understood and tested independently?
    - Is the implementation following the file structure from the plan?
    - Did this change create new files that are already large, or
      significantly grow existing files? (Don't flag pre-existing file
      sizes — focus on what this change contributed.)

    Your report should point at evidence: file:line references for every
    finding and for any check you would otherwise answer with a bare
    "yes." A tight report that cites lines gives the controller everything
    it needs.

    Your final message is the report itself: begin directly with the
    spec-compliance verdict. Every line is a verdict, a finding with
    file:line, or a check you ran — no preamble, no process narration,
    no closing summary.

    ## Calibration

    Categorize issues by actual severity. Not everything is Critical.
    Important means this task cannot be trusted until it is fixed: incorrect
    or fragile behavior, a missed requirement, or maintainability damage you
    would block a merge over — verbatim duplication of a logic block,
    swallowed errors, tests that assert nothing. "Coverage could be broader"
    and polish suggestions are Minor.
    If the plan or brief explicitly mandates something this rubric calls a
    defect (a test that asserts nothing, verbatim duplication of a logic
    block), that IS a finding — report it as Important, labeled
    plan-mandated. The plan's authorship does not grade its own work; the
    human decides.
    Acknowledge what was done well before listing issues — accurate praise
    helps the implementer trust the rest of the feedback.

    ## Output Format

    ### Spec Compliance

    - ✅ Spec compliant | ❌ Issues found: [what's missing/extra/misunderstood,
      with file:line references]
    - ⚠️ Cannot verify from diff: [requirements you could not verify from the
      diff alone, and what the controller should check — report alongside the
      ✅/❌ verdict for everything you could verify]

    ### Strengths
    [What's well done? Be specific.]

    ### Issues

    #### Critical (Must Fix)
    #### Important (Should Fix)
    #### Minor (Nice to Have)

    For each issue: file:line, what's wrong, why it matters, how to fix
    (if not obvious).

    ### Assessment

    **Task quality:** [Approved | Needs fixes]

    **Reasoning:** [1-2 sentence technical assessment]
```

**Placeholders:**
- `[MODEL]` — REQUIRED: reviewer model per SKILL.md Model Selection
- `[BRIEF_FILE]` — REQUIRED: the task brief file (`scripts/task-brief PLAN N`
  prints the path; same file the implementer worked from)
- `[GLOBAL_CONSTRAINTS]` — the binding requirements copied verbatim from
  the plan's Global Constraints section or the spec: exact values, formats,
  and stated relationships between components (not process rules — those
  are already in this template)
- `[REPORT_FILE]` — REQUIRED: the file the implementer wrote its detailed
  report to
- `[BASE_SHA]` — commit before this task
- `[HEAD_SHA]` — current commit
- `[DIFF_FILE]` — REQUIRED: the path the controller wrote the review
  package to (`scripts/review-package BASE HEAD` prints the unique path it
  wrote; the package never enters the controller's context)

**Reviewer returns:** Spec Compliance verdict (✅/❌/⚠️), Strengths, Issues
(Critical/Important/Minor), Task quality verdict

A fix dispatch can address spec gaps and quality findings together;
re-review after fixes covers both verdicts.

You are reviewing one task's implementation: first whether it matches its requirements, then whether it is well-built. This is a task-scoped gate, not a merge review — a broad whole-branch review happens separately after all tasks.

What Was Requested

Read the task brief: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/task-1-brief.md

Global constraints from the spec that bind this task:

  • Self-contained/offline tool; this task adds no dependencies.
  • Cache writes MUST keep the 5-minute / 1-hour split (priced differently later).
  • When only the aggregate cache_creation_input_tokens is present (no 5m/1h split), attribute the whole amount to the 5-minute (CacheWrite5m) bucket.
  • Usage is only carried on assistant records; user turns have nil Usage.
  • internal/transcript/raw.go must stay free of the internal/model import (return a local apiUsage); the model mapping happens in parse.go.
  • Attaching usage inside buildTurn (for rec.Type=="assistant") must cover BOTH main-chain and sidechain/subagent records.

What the Implementer Claims They Built

Read the implementer's report: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/task-1-report.md Treat it as unverified claims; verify against the diff. A stated rationale never downgrades a finding.

Diff Under Review

Base: 0bd5a25 Head: 1753ede Diff file: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/review-0bd5a25..1753ede.diff Read the diff file once — it is your view of the change (commit list + stat + full diff with context). Do not re-run git. Inspect code outside the diff only to evaluate a concrete named risk (one focused check per risk). Read-only; do not mutate the working tree.

Tests

The implementer already ran tests with TDD evidence for exactly this code. Do not re-run the suite. Run a focused test only if reading the code raises a specific doubt no existing run answers; otherwise name the test you would run. Non-pristine test output is a finding.

Specific things worth verifying by reading the diff:

  • The aggregate-cache-creation → 5m fallback only triggers when both split fields are zero AND the aggregate is > 0 (must not double-count when the split is present).
  • messageMeta returns nil Usage when the message has no usage object (so turns without usage get Usage == nil).
  • raw.go does not import internal/model.
  • The *a.Usage != want value-equality assertion in the test is valid (Usage is a comparable struct of ints).

Output Format

Follow the reviewer template exactly: begin directly with the Spec Compliance verdict (✅/❌/⚠️), then Strengths, then Issues (Critical/Important/Minor) each with file:line, then an Assessment with Task quality: Approved | Needs fixes. Every line a verdict, a finding with file:line, or a check you ran — no preamble or closing summary.

All checks confirmed clean. Compiling review conclusions.

Spec Compliance: ✅

The implementation fully satisfies task-1-brief.md and the global constraints:

  • model.Usage{Input, Output, CacheRead, CacheWrite5m, CacheWrite1h} and Turn.Model/Turn.Usage added exactly as specified (internal/model/model.go:49-61).
  • 5m/1h cache-write split preserved; aggregate cache_creation_input_tokens fallback only fires when both split fields are zero AND the aggregate is > 0 (internal/transcript/parse.go:173-176) — verified no double-counting when split is present (partial split, e.g. w1>0, w5=0, correctly skips the fallback).
  • Usage is nil for user turns (guarded by rec.Type == "assistant", internal/transcript/parse.go:221-223) and nil when the assistant message carries no usage object (decodeMessageMeta returns nil usage, messageMeta short-circuits, internal/transcript/raw.go:98-110, internal/transcript/parse.go:170-172).
  • internal/transcript/raw.go has no internal/model import (confirmed: only encoding/json, strings, time); model mapping lives in messageMeta in parse.go.
  • buildTurn is the single function invoked for both main-chain (parse.go:126) and sidechain/subagent (parse.go:116) records, so the Model/Usage attachment at parse.go:221-223 covers both paths.

Strengths

  • Clean separation: raw.go stays a pure JSON-decoding layer (local apiUsage struct); domain-level cache-split logic lives in parse.go's messageMeta, matching the brief's architecture intent.
  • Test coverage directly exercises both required behaviors: full usage with explicit split (TestParseAttachesModelAndUsage, parse_test.go:152-183) and aggregate-only fallback (TestParseAggregateCacheCreationDefaultsTo5m, parse_test.go:185-196), including the nil-Usage cases for user turns and assistant turns without a usage object.
  • *a.Usage != want is valid: model.Usage is a plain struct of five int fields, which is comparable in Go, so pointer-dereferenced value comparison with != is well-defined and correctly checks all fields at once.
  • Verified via a full-codebase check: no positional (unkeyed) model.Turn{} literals exist anywhere, so adding two trailing fields to Turn doesn't silently misassign values elsewhere; all other Turn/Usage references (render, redact, and their tests) use keyed literals or pointer field access and are unaffected.
  • No new dependencies introduced; only stdlib encoding/json (already used) is touched.

Issues

None found — no Critical, Important, or Minor issues.

(Noted but not flagged as a defect: messageMeta re-unmarshals rec.Message separately from decodeMessageContent's earlier unmarshal of the same raw bytes in Parse — a small double-parse per assistant record. This is the exact structure specified verbatim in the task brief's Step 4/5 code, not an implementer deviation, and is a non-functional micro-inefficiency rather than a correctness or spec-compliance problem.)

Assessment

Task Description

Read your task brief FIRST — it is your requirements, with the exact code and data to use verbatim: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/task-2-brief.md

Context

This is Task 2 of a token-usage & cost feature. You create a NEW package internal/usage that owns an embedded price table. Task 3 (a later task) will add cost computation in the same package and depends on the exact names you create here: usage.Price{Input, Output float64}, usage.Lookup(modelID string) (Price, bool), the const usage.PricesAsOf, and the unexported cache-multiplier consts cacheWrite5mMult=1.25, cacheWrite1hMult=2.0, cacheReadMult=0.1.

Notes:

  • The price data (prices.json) is embedded via go:embed. The prices in the brief are real Anthropic list prices captured 2026-07 — use them EXACTLY as given; do not "adjust" any number.
  • <synthetic> and any model not in the table must resolve as unpriced (Lookup returns false).
  • stripDateSuffix removes a trailing "-YYYYMMDD" (dash + 8 digits) so a dated model id resolves after a miss.
  • This is a brand-new package — no existing patterns to conflict with, but match the repo's Go style (package doc comment, gofmt).

The brief contains the complete prices.json, pricing.go, and pricing_test.go. Implement them verbatim (TDD: data + tests first per the brief's step order, then pricing.go, confirm green).

Your job

  1. Follow the brief's steps (create prices.json; write pricing_test.go; confirm it fails to compile/RED; implement pricing.go; confirm GREEN).
  2. Run go test ./internal/usage/ -v, and once before committing go build ./... && gofmt -l internal (expect no gofmt output).
  3. Commit exactly as the brief specifies.
  4. Self-review, then report.

Task 2 Review: Embedded Price Table

Spec Compliance: ✅ Fully compliant

Strengths

  • internal/usage/prices.json:1-31 — all 10 models match the brief's table exactly, entry-for-entry (opus-4-8/4-7/4-6/4-5 = 5/25, opus-4-1 = 15/75, sonnet-5 = 2/10, sonnet-4-6/4-5 = 3/15, haiku-4-5 = 1/5, fable-5 = 10/50). No <synthetic> key present, so it correctly falls through to unpriced.
  • internal/usage/pricing.go:99-103 — cache multiplier consts present with exact required values (cacheWrite5mMult=1.25, cacheWrite1hMult=2.0, cacheReadMult=0.1), unexported as specified, ready for Task 3 to consume.
  • internal/usage/pricing.go:113-117 — init() panics on json.Unmarshal failure rather than leaving prices nil, satisfying the fail-fast requirement.
  • internal/usage/pricing.go:133-148 — stripDateSuffix requires a leading - at tail[0] plus 8 all-digit characters; traced against "claude-opus-4-8" (last-9 = "-opus-4-8", second char 'o' fails the digit check → returned unchanged) and "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001" (last-9 = "-20251001", all digits → correctly stripped to "claude-haiku-4-5"). No corruption of undated ids.
  • internal/usage/pricing.go:51 — const PricesAsOf = "2026-07", a non-empty date string as required.
  • No go.mod changes in the diff — only stdlib embed/encoding/json used; self-contained, offline, no new dependencies.
  • pricing.go and pricing_test.go are verbatim transcriptions of the brief's Step 2/Step 4 code blocks — checked character-for-character, no drift.

Issues

None found — Critical: none. Important: none. Minor: none.

(Noted but not a defect: cacheWrite5mMult/cacheWrite1hMult/cacheReadMult are unused within this package's diff — expected, since Task 3 is the intended consumer, and Go does not flag unused package-level consts.)

  • Manually traced <synthetic> through stripDateSuffix (11 chars, tail[0]='y') confirming no accidental match.
  • Confirmed go.mod is untouched in the diff (no new dependencies introduced).
  • Did not re-run go test/go build — implementer's report already shows RED→GREEN TDD evidence and a clean gofmt -l, and the code read verbatim-matches the brief, so no specific doubt warranted a rerun.

AgentImplement Task 3: usage computationtranscript ↗

You are implementing Task 3: Usage computation — for the ccwhid Go project (module github.com/saigyo/cc-what-have-i-done).

Task Description

Read your task brief FIRST — it is your requirements, with the exact code and test cases to use verbatim: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/task-3-brief.md

Context

This is Task 3 of a token-usage & cost feature. You add the aggregation + cost math to the existing internal/usage package.

  • It consumes types created in earlier tasks: model.Session, model.Turn.Usage (*model.Usage), model.Turn.Model (from Task 1), and usage.Price, usage.Lookup(modelID) (Price, bool), the const usage.PricesAsOf, and unexported consts cacheWrite5mMult, cacheWrite1hMult, cacheReadMult (from Task 2, same package).
  • You produce: TokenCounts (with InOut() method), ModelUsage, Report, and Compute(s model.Session) Report.

Key correctness points (the tests in the brief enforce these — implement so they pass, do NOT alter the tests or prices):

  • Totals and per-model figures include nested subagent turns (walk into each Block's Tool.Subagents[].Turns recursively). Per-turn costs (PerTurnCost) are recorded for TOP-LEVEL turns only.
  • InOut() = Input + Output (excludes cache tokens).
  • Cost = inputinRate + outputoutRate + cacheWrite5minRate1.25 + cacheWrite1hinRate2.0 + cacheReadinRate0.1, where inRate = Price.Input/1e6, outRate = Price.Output/1e6.
  • Unpriced model (e.g. "") → CostUSD nil, sets HasUnknownModel; its tokens still count in Total but are excluded from TotalCostUSD. PerTurnCost[i] is present-with-nil for an unpriced assistant turn, and absent for user turns (nil Usage).
  • TotalCostUSD is nil only when nothing was priced.

The brief contains the complete usage.go and usage_test.go. Implement verbatim (TDD: write usage_test.go, confirm RED, implement usage.go, confirm GREEN).

Your job

  1. Follow the brief's TDD steps.
  2. Run go test ./internal/usage/ -v, then once before committing go build ./... && go vet ./internal/usage/ && gofmt -l internal (expect no gofmt output).
  3. Commit exactly as the brief specifies.
  4. Self-review, then report.

Work from: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done

If anything is ambiguous or seems wrong, STOP and report BLOCKED/NEEDS_CONTEXT rather than guessing.

Report

Write your full report (implemented, TDD RED/GREEN evidence with commands+output, files changed, self-review) to: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/task-3-report.md

Then reply with ONLY (under 15 lines): Status, commits (short SHA + subject), one-line test summary, concerns, and the report file path.

Verify specifically (think hard — this is the math/logic core):

  • The cost() formula matches the constraint exactly (inRate=Input/1e6; output uses Output/1e6; cache terms use inRate × the right multiplier). Hand-check against the brief test's expected 36.75 for opus-4-8 with 1e6 each of input/output/cacheRead/cacheWrite5m.
  • walk() recurses correctly into Block.Tool.Subagents[].Turns and does NOT double-count the top-level turn or miss nested turns; confirm the subagent test's Total.Input=2_000_000 and PerTurnCost has exactly 1 entry.
  • Totals accumulate over ALL turns (via walk) but PerTurnCost only over top-level s.Turns by index.
  • Per-model cost sums correctly; priced flips TotalCostUSD from nil only when at least one model is priced.
  • ByModel sort is stable and by InOut desc; unpriced models still appear (with nil cost) and set HasUnknownModel.
  • No float precision footguns that would break the exact-value assertions (they use round numbers, so exact equality within the test's tolerance should hold).
  • Check for any double-counting risk between the accumulate-walk pass and the per-model pass (they should be independent; per-model uses byModel map built during walk).

Output Format

Follow the reviewer template exactly: Spec Compliance verdict (✅/❌/⚠️) first, then Strengths, then Issues (Critical/Important/Minor) with file:line, then Assessment with Task quality: Approved | Needs fixes. Every line a verdict, a finding with file:line, or a check you ran.

Spec Compliance: ✅

All Report/TokenCounts/ModelUsage shapes, Compute semantics, and the four global constraints (nested-subagent inclusion in totals/per-model, top-level-only per-turn cost, InOut() excluding cache tokens, cache multipliers 1.25×/2×/0.1×, unknown-model nil-cost-but-counted-tokens, TotalCostUSD nil-only-when-nothing-priced, PerTurnCost present-with-nil vs. absent) are implemented exactly as specified in ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/internal/usage/usage.go.

Strengths

  • cost() (usage.go:50-56): formula hand-checked against the brief's worked example — inRate = p.Input/1e6; input 1e6×0.000005=$5, output 1e6×0.000025=$25, cache-read 1e6×0.000005×0.1=$0.50, 5m-write 1e6×0.000005×1.25=$6.25 → $36.75, matching TestComputeTotalsAndCost's expected value exactly. Re-ran the test in isolation: PASS.
  • walk() (usage.go:130-141): recurses into Block.Tool.Subagents[].Turns via st := range sub.Turns → walk(st, fn), calling fn exactly once per turn (top-level via the for _, t := range s.Turns driver loop, nested via recursion) — no double counting, no missed nesting. Confirmed against TestComputeCountsSubagentTurnsInTotals: Total.Input = 2,000,000 (1e6 top-level + 1e6 nested) and len(PerTurnCost) == 1 (top-level only), both passing.
  • Totals/per-model accumulation (usage.go:60-77) walks all turns via accumulate; per-turn cost (usage.go:80-91) iterates only s.Turns by index and skips nil-Usage turns entirely (map key absent) vs. explicit r.PerTurnCost[i] = nil for unpriced-but-present usage — matches the present-with-nil vs. absent distinction precisely.
  • Per-model pass (usage.go:94-108) reads from the byModel map already built during the walk pass — no re-traversal of the session, so no double-counting risk between the two passes. priced bool correctly gates TotalCostUSD from nil.
  • ByModel sort (usage.go:110-112) uses sort.SliceStable descending by InOut(); unpriced models still appended to ByModel with nil CostUSD and set HasUnknownModel (usage.go:100-103).
  • Verified independently: go build ./... succeeds, go vet ./internal/usage/ clean, gofmt -l internal/usage/ reports no files, all 7 tests in the package pass with pristine (non-flaky) output, confirmed by re-running the full package suite and the specific hand-check test in isolation.
  • Confirmed via a research subagent that model.Turn.Usage (*Usage), model.Block.Tool (*ToolCall), model.ToolCall.Subagents ([]Subagent), and model.Subagent.Turns ([]Turn) exactly match the types usage.go assumes, with correct nil-guards (t.Usage == nil, b.Tool == nil) in place.

Issues

None found — Critical/Important/Minor: none.

Assessment

The diff is a verbatim, faithful transcription of the brief's Step 3 code with no deviations. Math hand-check, subagent-recursion hand-check, sort/nil-handling logic, and independent build/vet/gofmt/test verification all confirm correctness against every stated global constraint.

You are implementing Task 4: Render the usage card and per-turn badges — for the ccwhid Go project (module github.com/saigyo/cc-what-have-i-done).

Task Description

Read your task brief FIRST — it is your requirements, with the exact code to use verbatim: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/task-4-brief.md

Context

This is Task 4 of a token-usage & cost feature. It wires the internal/usage package (built in Tasks 2–3) into the HTML renderer.

  • Consumes: usage.Compute(s model.Session) usage.Report, usage.Report (fields Total, TotalCostUSD *float64, ByModel []usage.ModelUsage, PerTurnCost map[int]*float64, PricesAsOf, HasUnknownModel, HasAnyUsage), usage.ModelUsage{Model, Tokens usage.TokenCounts, CostUSD *float64}, usage.TokenCounts.InOut(). Also model.Turn.Usage / .Model from Task 1.
  • Produces: a new render.Options.Usage bool field; a usage card + per-turn badges in the generated HTML, gated on that option.

Files you create/modify (per brief): create internal/render/format.go + internal/render/format_test.go; modify internal/render/render.go (add usage import, Options.Usage, viewData.Usage, turnView.Badge, usageView/usageRow/usageModel types, change Site to pass opts into buildViewModel, rewrite buildViewModel, add turnBadge + buildUsageView); modify internal/render/assets/report.html.tmpl (usage card after , badge span in turn-role); append to internal/render/assets/styles.css; add tests to internal/render/render_test.go.

Critical repo facts:

  • render.go's buildViewModel is currently buildViewModel(s model.Session, title string) viewData and is called only from Site. You change its signature to add opts Options and update the Site call site.
  • The embedded assets (report.html.tmpl, styles.css, app.js) are served via go:embed; keep them self-contained/offline — NO external refs (no http/CDN/url()/@font-face) in the CSS you add.
  • render_test.go already imports os, path/filepath, strings, model. If you add a readIndex helper, make sure it doesn't duplicate an existing one.
  • Existing tests must keep passing (they render without the usage option; the card must be ABSENT unless Options.Usage is set).

The brief has the complete code for every file. Implement verbatim, following the brief's TDD ordering (format_test → format.go; then render viewmodel/template/css; then render_test integration tests).

Your job

  1. Follow the brief's steps in order.
  2. Run go test ./internal/render/ -v. Before committing, run the full suite go build ./... && go vet ./... && gofmt -l internal && go test ./... (expect gofmt no output, all green).
  3. Commit exactly as the brief specifies.
  4. Self-review (especially: card absent without --usage; badge only on turns with usage; no external refs in CSS), then report.

Work from: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done

AgentReview Task 4 (spec + quality)transcript ↗

You are reviewing one task's implementation: first whether it matches its requirements, then whether it is well-built. Task-scoped gate.

What Was Requested

Read the task brief: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/task-4-brief.md

Global constraints that bind this task:

  • Opt-in: the usage card/badges render ONLY when Options.Usage is true; absent otherwise. Existing render tests (no usage option) must still pass with no card.
  • Self-contained/offline: the CSS added to styles.css must have NO external refs (no http/CDN, no url(), no @font-face).
  • Collapsed headline counts input+output only ("in+out"); expanded shows token breakdown + per-model table + footnote with "prices as of " and, when HasUnknownModel, a note that totals exclude unpriced models.
  • Per-turn badge only on turns that have usage; unpriced turns show tokens with no cost.
  • Redaction is untouched by this task.

Implementer's Report (unverified — verify against the diff). Note the implementer DEVIATED from the brief.

~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/task-4-report.md

Diff Under Review

Base: 4140bfb Head: 4de9666 Diff file: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/review-4140bfb..4de9666.diff Read the diff file once. Do not re-run git. Read-only. Inspect outside the diff only for a concrete named risk.

PRIORITY: scrutinize the deviation

The brief specified usageView.Headline string and usageModel.Tokens string (plain strings, auto-escaped by html/template). The implementer changed BOTH to template.HTML built via html.EscapeString(...), because html/template escapes the literal + in "in+out" to &#43;, which broke the strings.Contains(html, "in+out") test.

Judge this rigorously:

  1. XSS/injection safety: For EACH value now typed template.HTML, trace every input that composes it. Does any session-derived / user-controlled data (model id, or anything from the transcript) flow into a template.HTML field WITHOUT html.EscapeString? The model id (usageModel.Model) is session-derived — confirm it stays a plain string (auto-escaped) and is NOT concatenated into the template.HTML Tokens/Headline. Numbers from formatTokens/formatCost are digits/./k/M/$/< — confirm they cannot carry markup. Flag as Critical any path where transcript data reaches raw template.HTML unescaped.
  2. Was a simpler/safer fix available? e.g. keeping plain strings and changing the headline text to avoid + (like "in/out"), or asserting the escaped form in the test. Note it as a Minor suggestion if the template.HTML approach is safe but heavier than necessary. Do NOT block on this if the deviation is safe.

Also verify

  • Card is gated on Options.Usage (absent when false) — confirm both the template {{ with .Usage }} guard AND that buildViewModel only sets d.Usage when opts.Usage. Confirm TestSiteOmitsUsageByDefault asserts absence.
  • Per-turn badge only set when opts.Usage && t.Usage != nil.
  • CSS additions have no external refs (grep the diff for http, url(, @font-face, @import).
  • buildViewModel signature change is consistent with the Site call site; no other callers.
  • The no-data case (HasAnyUsage false) renders the note, not an empty broken card.
  • Tests assert real behavior (card present with usage, absent without, badge present, model id present, "prices as of" present).

Tests

Implementer ran the full suite green. Don't re-run the whole suite; a focused test only for a specific doubt, else name it. Non-pristine output is a finding.

Output Format

Follow the reviewer template exactly: Spec Compliance verdict (✅/❌/⚠️) first, then Strengths, then Issues (Critical/Important/Minor) with file:line, then Assessment with Task quality: Approved | Needs fixes. Be decisive about the deviation: is it safe (Approved) or a real injection risk (Needs fixes)?

All confirmed. Here is the review.

Spec Compliance: ✅

The implementation matches the brief's requirements. The one deviation (typing usageView.Headline and usageModel.Tokens as template.HTML instead of plain string) is a safe, narrowly-scoped fix for a genuine html/template auto-escaping issue (+ → &#43;), not a shortcut that introduces risk.

Strengths

  • The deviation reuses an established, pre-existing convention in the same file: renderTool (render.go:220 onward) already does html.EscapeString(...) before building raw HTML strings for tool names/summaries/results. The fix is consistent with house style, not a novel pattern.
  • usageModel.Model — the one session/transcript-derived value in this view model (confirmed via internal/transcript/raw.go that model IDs are decoded from JSONL with no sanitization) — was correctly left as a plain string, so html/template's auto-escaper still protects it. It is never concatenated into a template.HTML field.
  • Both template.HTML fields are traceably built only from formatTokens/formatCost output (digits, k, M, $, ., <) and hardcoded literal words, then passed through html.EscapeString before the cast — this correctly neutralizes the one dangerous character these formatters can emit (formatCost's "<$0.01").
  • Report.PricesAsOf is a hardcoded Go constant (internal/usage/pricing.go:15), not transcript-derived — confirmed safe regardless of type.
  • Gating is correct and verified end-to-end: buildViewModel only calls usage.Compute/buildUsageView and sets d.Usage when opts.Usage (render.go ~121-124); template uses {{ with .Usage }} (nil-safe); the sole external caller (cmd/ccwhid/run.go:99) doesn't set Usage, so pre-existing CLI behavior is unchanged. TestSiteOmitsUsageByDefault asserts absence of "usage-card".
  • Per-turn badge correctly gated: tv.Badge set only if opts.Usage && t.Usage != nil.
  • CSS additions grep clean for http, url(, @font-face, @import — fully self-contained, using only pre-existing var(--border/--card, ...) fallbacks and literals.
  • buildViewModel signature change has exactly one call site (render.go:41 within Site); no other internal or external callers were missed.
  • TokenCounts.InOut() (usage.go:20) sums only Input+Output, confirmed excluding cache tokens — the collapsed headline correctly reflects "in+out only" per the constraint.
  • No-data case (HasAnyUsage == false) matches the brief's own documented behavior exactly: headline conveys state, tables hidden via {{ if .HasAny }}, empty <p> for footnote — this was explicitly called out as acceptable in the brief itself (Step 6 note), not a defect introduced by the implementer.
  • Redaction untouched — diff touches only render.go/render_test.go/format.go/format_test.go/template/CSS.

Issues

Critical: None.

Important: None.

Minor:

  • internal/render/render.go:159 — the no-data headline (template.HTML("Usage · no token-usage data")) is a hardcoded literal, so it's safe, but it skips the html.EscapeString wrapper the rest of the code uses, which is a small stylistic inconsistency (harmless here only because the string is a fixed literal with no interpolation — if anyone later edits this line to interpolate a variable without noticing the missing escape step, that would become a real risk). Consider routing it through the same html.EscapeString call for uniformity/future-proofing.
  • The template.HTML fix is safe but heavier than necessary: an equally valid, lighter-weight alternative existed (e.g., have buildUsageView split the "+" out with template.HTMLEscapeString-free plain concatenation isn't possible in html/template, but the brief's own test string "in+out" was the actual constraint forcing this). Given the brief hardcodes the literal test assertion strings.Contains(html, "in+out"), the implementer's options were limited to (a) template.HTML conversion (what was done) or (b) asserting the escaped form "in&#43;out" in the test (a brief deviation too). The implementer's choice is defensible and no less brief-compliant than the alternative — not a blocking finding.
  • No automated test exercises the "Options.Usage: true but no turn has usage" (HasAnyUsage == false) path in render_test.go — only the two brief-specified tests (enabled-with-data, disabled) exist. This gap pre-exists in the brief's own Step 8 spec, not introduced by the implementer, but worth noting for future coverage.

Assessment

The deviation was scrutinized end-to-end: every input composing the two template.HTML fields was traced back to formatTokens/formatCost (digit/./k/M/$/< only) and hardcoded literal words, each passed through html.EscapeString before the cast. The one session-derived value in the whole feature (usageModel.Model, sourced from unsanitized transcript message.model) was correctly kept as a plain auto-escaped string and never touches the template.HTML fields. No transcript/session/user data reaches raw HTML unescaped. All other brief constraints (opt-in gating, CSS self-containment, collapsed/expanded content rules, per-turn badge gating, redaction untouched) verified directly against the diff and cross-checked against internal/usage/usage.go, internal/transcript/raw.go, and the CLI call site.

You are implementing Task 5: CLI flag, TUI toggle, and docs — the final task of the usage & cost feature for ccwhid (module github.com/saigyo/cc-what-have-i-done).

Task Description

Read your task brief FIRST — it is your requirements, with the exact code to use verbatim: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/task-5-brief.md

Context

Tasks 1–4 added parsing, pricing, computation, and rendering of a usage section gated on render.Options.Usage bool. Your task exposes it to users:

  • CLI: add a --usage bool flag (default false) to cmd/ccwhid/main.go's options struct + newRootCmd, and thread it into the render.Site(..., render.Options{Title:..., Usage: opts.usage}) call in cmd/ccwhid/run.go's generate.
  • TUI: add an "Include usage & cost" toggle to the options screen in internal/tui/tui.go (new optUsage index, Selection.Usage field, toggle case, and a row in the toggles slice), and map opts.usage = sel.Usage in main.go's run() where other sel fields are applied.
  • README: add the --usage flags-table row and a "Token usage & cost" section.

CRITICAL repo facts to reconcile with the brief (an earlier feature already extended the TUI options screen, so the brief's option-index block may not match verbatim):

  • internal/tui/tui.go currently has an options-screen index block with iota constants. Check the CURRENT constants — they already include an output-dir row and generate row, e.g. optSubagents, optRedact, optOpen, optOutDir, optGenerate, optionCount. You must insert optUsage in the RIGHT position so it stays consistent with the toggles slice order in viewOptions. The toggles slice currently renders {subagents, redact, open} then an output-dir row then generate. Insert the usage toggle as the 4th toggle (after open, before the output-dir row), i.e. index order optSubagents, optRedact, optOpen, optUsage, optOutDir, optGenerate, optionCount, and add {"Include usage & cost", m.sel.Usage} as the 4th entry in the toggles slice. Verify the toggle-index → toggles[i] mapping and the updateOptions switch cases stay aligned after inserting.
  • internal/tui/tui_test.go has helpers newModel, testGroups, send, key. The brief's TestOptionsUsageToggle navigates down to optUsage — make sure the number of key("down") presses matches the actual cursor position of the usage row given the real layout (usage is the 4th row, index optUsage=3, so 3 downs from index 0). Adjust the test's down-count if the brief's assumed layout differs from the real one, keeping the assertion that the cursor lands on optUsage and space toggles sel.Usage.
  • Existing TUI tests (output-dir input, toggles, navigation) MUST still pass — the inserted row shifts later indices, so re-run the whole tui package.

Implement the brief, adapting the TUI index/insertion to the ACTUAL current file. Follow TDD for the TUI toggle test.

Your job

  1. Apply CLI changes (main.go options+flag+sel mapping; run.go Site call).
  2. Apply TUI changes; run go test ./internal/tui/ -v (new + existing must pass).
  3. Update README.
  4. Full verification: gofmt -l . && go vet ./... && go build ./... && go test ./... — expect no gofmt output, all green. Also sanity-check go run ./cmd/ccwhid --help | grep usage shows the flag.
  5. Commit as the brief specifies.
  6. Self-review, then report.

Work from: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done

AgentReview Task 5 (spec + quality)transcript ↗

You are reviewing one task's implementation: first whether it matches its requirements, then whether it is well-built. Task-scoped gate (this is the final task).

What Was Requested

Read the task brief: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/task-5-brief.md

Global constraints that bind this task:

  • Opt-in: --usage defaults to false; the section renders only when set.
  • CLI --usage maps to render.Options.Usage; TUI toggle maps to Selection.Usage → opts.usage.
  • The TUI options screen already had rows (subagents, redact, open, output-dir, generate) from a prior feature; the new usage toggle had to be inserted WITHOUT breaking the existing index↔row alignment.

Implementer's Report (unverified — verify against the diff)

~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/task-5-report.md

Diff Under Review

Base: 4de9666 Head: 6fef39b Diff file: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/review-4de9666..6fef39b.diff Read the diff once. Do not re-run git. Read-only. Inspect outside the diff only for a concrete named risk.

PRIORITY: verify the TUI index↔row alignment (the main risk)

The TUI options screen maps a cursor index to both (a) a toggles[i] slice entry in viewOptions and (b) a case opt* in the updateOptions space/enter switch. Inserting optUsage shifts optOutDir/optGenerate. Verify RIGOROUSLY:

  1. The const (optSubagents … optionCount) iota block order matches the toggles slice order (subagents, redact, open, usage) AND the output-dir/generate handling. Specifically: is optUsage inserted at the same ordinal position (4th, index 3) as the "Include usage & cost" entry in the toggles slice?
  2. The updateOptions " ", "enter" switch: does case optUsage toggle m.sel.Usage, and do optOutDir/optGenerate cases still map to the correct (shifted) actions? Confirm no case now points at the wrong row.
  3. viewOptions renders the toggles slice by index, then the output-dir row keyed on optOutDir, then generate keyed on optGenerate — confirm these keys still equal the intended rows after the shift.
  4. The new TestOptionsUsageToggle: does its number of key("down") presses actually land the cursor on optUsage (index 3 → 3 downs from 0), and does the existing output-dir test (if it navigates by down-count) still land correctly given the inserted row? If the existing output-dir test's down-count was NOT updated but the row moved, that's a real breakage — check whether it still passes by construction.

Also verify

  • cmd/ccwhid/main.go: --usage flag registered (default false); opts.usage mapped from sel.Usage in the TUI branch of run().
  • cmd/ccwhid/run.go: render.Site call passes Usage: opts.usage.
  • Selection.Usage field added.
  • README: flags-table row for --usage and a "Token usage & cost" section; content accurate (opt-in, estimate, unknown→n/a, offline).
  • Existing tests still pass (report claims 10 tui tests green + full suite). Non-pristine output is a finding.

Tests

Implementer ran the full suite green. Don't re-run the whole suite; a focused test only for a specific doubt (e.g. if you suspect an index misalignment the tests wouldn't catch), else name it.

Output Format

Follow the reviewer template: Spec Compliance (✅/❌/⚠️) first, then Strengths, Issues (Critical/Important/Minor) with file:line, then Assessment with Task quality: Approved | Needs fixes.

Spec Compliance: ✅

All requirements of task-5-brief.md are met exactly as specified.

Strengths

  • Index alignment is correct and robust. const (optSubagents, optRedact, optOpen, optUsage, optOutDir, optGenerate, optionCount) — optUsage sits at ordinal 3, matching the 4th (index-3) entry appended to the toggles slice (internal/tui/tui.go:210-218,372-380). The render loop uses m.optCursor == i against the slice index, and updateOptions's switch uses named constants (case optOutDir, case optGenerate) rather than hardcoded integers (internal/tui/tui.go:259-273), so the shift propagates safely with no dangling magic numbers. Navigation bound (optionCount-1) also updates automatically.
  • Existing tests correctly repaired for the shift. TestOptionsOutputDirInput and TestEditingModeTakesLiteralKeys both got their down-count bumped from 3→4 to keep landing on optOutDir (now index 4). Verified by running: all 10 TUI tests pass, including the new TestOptionsUsageToggle (3 downs → optUsage, index 3 — correct).
  • Wiring is complete end-to-end: --usage flag (default false) → opts.usage → sel.Usage (TUI branch) → render.Options{Usage: opts.usage} in run.go:144. render.Options.Usage field already exists from Task 4 (internal/render/render.go:22).
  • README accurate: flags-table row added verbatim, and the new "Token usage & cost" section correctly describes opt-in behavior, exact token counts, estimated cost, n/a for unknown models, and the offline guarantee.
  • Verified independently: go build ./..., go vet ./..., gofmt -l . all clean; go test ./... all packages pass.

Issues

None found at Critical, Important, or Minor severity that block this task. One trivial observation, not a defect: the pre-existing TestRootCmdHasExpectedFlags in cmd/ccwhid/main_test.go was not extended to assert the usage flag name is registered (it lists session, project, latest, out, title, include-subagents, no-redact, force, open but not usage). This is outside the brief's explicit step list and the flag's presence is otherwise verified (report shows --help output), so it's not worth blocking on — flagging only as an optional follow-up.

Assessment

The implementer correctly recognized that the repo's actual tui.go layout (with pre-existing optOutDir/optGenerate rows) diverged from the brief's simplified sketch, and resolved it exactly as the brief anticipated — inserting optUsage before optOutDir and fixing the two tests whose down-counts were affected by the shift. All index↔row mappings (const block, toggles slice, switch cases, view rendering) are consistent and use named constants throughout, eliminating off-by-one risk. CLI/TUI/render wiring and README are all accurate and complete.

# Code Reviewer Prompt Template

Use this template when dispatching a code reviewer subagent.

**Purpose:** Review completed work against requirements and code quality standards before it cascades into more work.

```
Subagent (general-purpose):
  description: "Review code changes"
  prompt: |
    You are a Senior Code Reviewer with expertise in software architecture,
    design patterns, and best practices. Your job is to review completed work
    against its plan or requirements and identify issues before they cascade.

    ## What Was Implemented

    [DESCRIPTION]

    ## Requirements / Plan

    [PLAN_OR_REQUIREMENTS]

    ## Git Range to Review

    **Base:** [BASE_SHA]
    **Head:** [HEAD_SHA]

    ```bash
    git diff --stat [BASE_SHA]..[HEAD_SHA]
    git diff [BASE_SHA]..[HEAD_SHA]
    ```

    ## Read-Only Review

    Your review is read-only on this checkout. Do not mutate the working tree, the index, HEAD, or branch state in any way. Use tools like `git show`, `git diff`, and `git log` to inspect history. If you need a working copy of a different revision, check it out into a separate temporary directory (e.g. `git worktree add /tmp/review-[SHA] [SHA]`) — never move HEAD on this checkout.

    ## What to Check

    **Plan alignment:**
    - Does the implementation match the plan / requirements?
    - Are deviations justified improvements, or problematic departures?
    - Is all planned functionality present?

    **Code quality:**
    - Clean separation of concerns?
    - Proper error handling?
    - Type safety where applicable?
    - DRY without premature abstraction?
    - Edge cases handled?

    **Architecture:**
    - Sound design decisions?
    - Reasonable scalability and performance?
    - Security concerns?
    - Integrates cleanly with surrounding code?

    **Testing:**
    - Tests verify real behavior, not mocks?
    - Edge cases covered?
    - Integration tests where they matter?
    - All tests passing?

    **Production readiness:**
    - Migration strategy if schema changed?
    - Backward compatibility considered?
    - Documentation complete?
    - No obvious bugs?

    ## Calibration

    Categorize issues by actual severity. Not everything is Critical.
    Acknowledge what was done well before listing issues — accurate praise
    helps the implementer trust the rest of the feedback.

    If you find significant deviations from the plan, flag them specifically
    so the implementer can confirm whether the deviation was intentional.
    If you find issues with the plan itself rather than the implementation,
    say so.

    ## Output Format

    ### Strengths
    [What's well done? Be specific.]

    ### Issues

    #### Critical (Must Fix)
    [Bugs, security issues, data loss risks, broken functionality]

    #### Important (Should Fix)
Claude2k tok · ~$0.28

Dispatching the final whole-branch review on opus:

Claude
AgentFinal whole-branch reviewtranscript ↗

You are a Senior Code Reviewer doing the final whole-branch review of a completed feature before merge. Be rigorous and adversarial: find real defects, not style nits.

What Was Implemented

An opt-in token-usage & estimated-cost section for ccwhid (a Go CLI that renders a Claude Code session transcript into a self-contained HTML report). Built across 5 tasks: (1) parse per-turn model+usage; (2) embedded price table; (3) usage aggregation + cost math (new internal/usage); (4) render a collapsible usage card + per-turn badges; (5) --usage CLI flag + TUI toggle + README. Each task already passed an independent task-level review; this is the cross-cutting pass.

Requirements / Design

Spec: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-12-usage-cost-section-design.md Plan: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-12-usage-cost.md Read both. Key invariants to check the WHOLE feature against:

  • Opt-in: nothing renders/among cost unless --usage/TUI toggle set.
  • Offline/self-contained: no runtime network; no new deps; no external refs in embedded CSS/assets.
  • Cost is an estimate from an embedded dated table; unknown/<synthetic> model → tokens counted, cost n/a, excluded from totals; PricesAsOf surfaced.
  • Cache multipliers 1.25×/2×/0.1× off base input; aggregate cache_creation (no split) → 5m bucket.
  • Collapsed headline counts input+output only (not cache).
  • Totals include nested subagent turns; per-turn badges top-level only.
  • Redaction untouched by this feature.

Git Range to Review

Base: fcd7fbd Head: 6fef39b Review package (commit list + stat + full diff with context), read it once: ~/IdeaProjects/cc-what-have-i-done/.superpowers/sdd/review-fcd7fbd..6fef39b.diff (The range includes two docs-only commits for the spec/plan — focus on the code.) You may also Read the actual current files to judge integration and to inspect code just outside the diff for a concrete named risk. Read-only: do not mutate the working tree/index/HEAD.

Cross-cutting things the per-task reviews could NOT fully see — check these hardest

  1. End-to-end correctness of a real cost number. Trace one assistant turn's usage → parse → Compute → render. Do the numbers that reach the HTML make sense? Any unit error (per-MTok vs per-token), double-counting between per-model and totals, or between top-level and subagent turns?
  2. The Task-4 deviation: usageView.Headline and usageModel.Tokens are template.HTML. Confirm across the whole render path that NO transcript-derived/user data (esp. model id) reaches a template.HTML field unescaped. Confirm the model id stays a plain auto-escaped string.
  3. Interaction with existing features: does the usage card/badge markup interact badly with the client-side filter/expand-collapse/theme JS (app.js) or the existing CSS? Does adding a <details> usage card or the badge span break the prompt sidebar, turn anchors, or the "Hide tool detail" toggle? (Inspect app.js/styles.css if needed.)
  4. --include-subagents=false interaction: when subagents are excluded from rendering, are their tokens correctly ALSO excluded from usage totals (usage.Compute walks session.Turns which already omit excluded subagents), so the displayed total matches what's shown? Confirm there's no mismatch where totals count turns that aren't rendered.
  5. Redaction ordering: generate() redacts the session then renders. Usage numbers aren't secret, but confirm redaction doesn't corrupt model ids or usage (it shouldn't touch them) and that the usage path reads the right (post-redaction) session.
  6. Empty/degenerate inputs: a session with zero assistant turns, or all-synthetic, or --usage with a transcript that has no usage at all — does anything panic, divide by zero, or render a broken card?

Known minors already logged (don't re-litigate; confirm they're truly minor or escalate if worse than thought)

  • render.go no-data headline literal skips html.EscapeString (safe literal).
  • template.HTML fix heavier than a plain-string label without "+".
  • No render test for --usage + no-usage-data path.
  • messageMeta re-parses rec.Message (micro double-parse).
  • main_test.go doesn't assert the usage flag is registered.

Output Format

Strengths (specific)

Issues → #### Critical / #### Important / #### Minor — each with file:line, the concrete failure scenario (inputs → wrong output/panic), and a fix.

Assessment: is the branch ready to merge? (Ready | Needs fixes) with one-line reasoning.

Be decisive. If you assert something is safe, say what you checked.

Confirmed: redaction touches only block text, tool fields, and subagent descriptions/blocks — never Turn.Model or Turn.Usage. The usage path reads correct post-redaction values.

Here is my cross-cutting review.

Strengths (specific)

  • Cost math is unit-correct and non-double-counting. cost() in internal/usage/usage.go:49 divides every rate by 1e6 (per-MTok → per-token) and applies the cache multipliers (1.25/2.0/0.1) off p.Input exactly as the spec requires. Traced end-to-end: opus turn with 1M each of in/out/cache-read/cache-write-5m → $5 + $25 + $0.50 + $6.25 = $36.75 (matches the test). Totals (r.Total) and per-model buckets are accumulated independently; TotalCostUSD is the sum of per-model priced costs only, so there is no cross-counting between the totals path and the per-model path, nor between top-level and subagent turns.
  • Subagent totals stay consistent with what's rendered. --include-subagents=false short-circuits in parse.go:113 and never seeds tool.Subagents (parse.go:139), so usage.walk (usage.go:131) finds no nested turns to count. Totals therefore match the rendered content in both modes — no phantom tokens. Verified by reading both the parse gate and the walk.
  • The Task-4 template.HTML deviation is safe. Headline and usageModel.Tokens are template.HTML but are built solely from html.EscapeString(...) over our own formatted numbers and literal words (render.go:169,178). The transcript-derived model id reaches only usageModel.Model, a plain string rendered as <td>{{ .Model }}</td> and thus auto-escaped by html/template. No user/transcript data reaches an unescaped template.HTML sink. Confirmed by tracing every field.
  • Degenerate inputs are safe. Zero-turn / all-synthetic / no-usage sessions produce a non-nil d.Usage, the no-data headline, and hidden tables ({{ if .HasAny }}); no division, no nil-deref, no panic. formatTokens/formatCost have no divide-by-zero paths.
  • Offline guarantee held. No new deps; the CSS additions use no url()/@font-face/external refs; prices are go:embed. Full suite, go vet, and gofmt -l are all clean.

Issues

Important

1. Usage card is unreadable in dark mode — background uses an undefined CSS variable. internal/render/assets/styles.css:69

.usage-card { ... background: var(--card, #fff); }

--card is defined nowhere in the stylesheet (only --surface, --bg, --text, etc. exist — verified by grep). So the background always resolves to the #fff fallback. The card's summary/body/table text sets no color and inherits body { color: var(--text) }, which in dark mode (prefers-color-scheme: dark or data-theme="dark") is #ECEAE2 (near-white). Result: near-white text on a white card — the headline, token breakdown, and per-model table are effectively invisible for any user whose OS/report is in dark mode. This is the feature's primary output rendering unusable by default, with no user action, on a very common setting. Every other surface in the report correctly uses var(--surface). Failure scenario: ccwhid --usage, open the report on a dark-mode OS → the Usage card is a white box with unreadable text. Fix: use the existing theme token — background: var(--surface); (drop the bogus --card/#fff fallback; --border in the same rule is fine since it is defined).

Minor

2. Hardcoded muted colors bypass theme tokens. styles.css:75-76 set .usage-note and .usage-badge to a literal #8a8578 instead of var(--muted). Legible on both surfaces so not broken, but inconsistent with the rest of the file and won't track a future palette change. Fix: color: var(--muted);. (Bundle with #1.)

3. Per-model "tokens" column excludes cache while its "cost" includes cache — potentially confusing. render.go:178 shows formatTokens(m.Tokens.InOut()) + " in+out", but m.CostUSD includes cache read/write cost. A model whose cost is dominated by cache (common) will show e.g. 10k in+out · $4.80, which looks like a wildly high per-token rate. Consistent with the "in+out headline" convention and not wrong, but the column label "tokens" over-promises. Consider labeling the header "in+out" or adding cache to the per-model line. Non-blocking.

Confirmations on the pre-logged minors

  • render.go no-data headline literal (render.go:159) skips html.EscapeString — safe, it's a constant literal. Confirmed truly minor.
  • template.HTML on Headline/Tokens is heavier than needed (a plain string would be auto-escaped identically, and + is not escaped in HTML text context by either path) — unnecessary but safe, not a defect.
  • messageMeta re-parsing rec.Message after decodeMessageContent — micro double-parse, negligible.
  • No render test for --usage + no-usage-data path — behavior is nonetheless safe (verified by reading the path); low risk.
  • main_test.go not asserting --usage registration — cosmetic; the flag is registered at main.go:118 and threaded through run.go:99.

Assessment: Needs fixes — one Important dark-mode defect (--card undefined → unreadable usage card in dark mode, styles.css:69); the logic, escaping, subagent/total consistency, and redaction paths are all correct and merge-ready once the CSS is switched to var(--surface).

26:body { margin: 0; background: var(--bg); color: var(--text); font-family: var(--font); line-height: 1.6; }
27:.topbar { position: sticky; top: 0; z-index: 10; background: var(--surface); border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border); }
29:.brand { font-weight: 700; color: var(--accent); letter-spacing: .02em; }
30:#filter { flex: 1; padding: .4rem .6rem; border: 1px solid var(--border); border-radius: 8px; background: var(--bg); color: var(--text); }
31:.btn { padding: .4rem .7rem; border: 1px solid var(--border); border-radius: 8px; background: var(--bg); color: var(--text); cursor: pointer; font-size: .85rem; }
32:.btn:hover { border-color: var(--accent); }
35:.sidebar-title { font-size: .75rem; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: .08em; color: var(--muted); margin-bottom: .5rem; }
38:.prompt-list a { color: var(--text); text-decoration: none; font-size: .85rem; display: block; padding: .25rem .4rem; border-radius: 6px; }
39:.prompt-list a::before { content: counter(p) ". "; color: var(--muted); }
40:.prompt-list a:hover { background: var(--accent-soft); }
43:.meta { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: .75rem; color: var(--muted); font-size: .85rem; margin-bottom: 1.5rem; }
44:.turn { background: var(--surface); border: 1px solid var(--border); border-radius: 12px; padding: 1rem 1.2rem; margin-bottom: 1rem; scroll-margin-top: 4.5rem; }
45:.turn-user { background: var(--user-bg); border-color: var(--accent-soft); }
46:.turn-role { font-size: .7rem; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: .08em; color: var(--muted); margin-bottom: .5rem; }
47:.turn-user .turn-role { color: var(--accent); }
50:.turn-body pre { background: var(--code-bg); padding: .8rem; border-radius: 8px; overflow-x: auto; font-family: var(--mono); font-size: .82rem; }
51:.turn-body code { font-family: var(--mono); font-size: .9em; }
52:.tool { border: 1px solid var(--border); border-radius: 8px; margin: .6rem 0; background: var(--bg); }
54:.tool-name { font-weight: 600; color: var(--accent); font-size: .8rem; }
55:.tool-summary { font-family: var(--mono); font-size: .8rem; color: var(--muted); overflow: hidden; text-overflow: ellipsis; white-space: nowrap; }
57:.tool-result { color: var(--text); }
58:.tool-result-error { border-left: 3px solid var(--accent); }
59:.thinking { color: var(--muted); font-size: .9rem; margin: .5rem 0; }
61:.subagent { margin: .6rem 0; border-left: 3px solid var(--accent-soft); padding-left: .7rem; }
62:.subagent summary { cursor: pointer; color: var(--accent); font-size: .85rem; }
63:.diff { font-family: var(--mono); font-size: .8rem; background: var(--code-bg); border-radius: 8px; padding: .5rem; overflow-x: auto; }
69:.usage-card { margin: 0 0 1.5rem; border: 1px solid var(--border, #e5e2d8); border-radius: 8px; background: var(--card, #fff); }
70:.usage-summary { cursor: pointer; padding: 0.6rem 0.9rem; font-weight: 600; }
71:.usage-body { padding: 0 0.9rem 0.9rem; font-size: 0.9rem; }
72:.usage-tokens td:last-child, .usage-models td, .usage-models th { text-align: right; padding: 0.1rem 0.6rem; }
73:.usage-tokens td:first-child, .usage-models td:first-child { text-align: left; }
74:.usage-models { margin-top: 0.6rem; border-collapse: collapse; }
